answer:
Debugging Jest Unit Tests

1. Definition & Context

Debugging Jest unit tests involves systematically diagnosing why tests fail, produce false positives, or behave inconsistently. Common issues include incorrect assertions, async timing bugs, shared mutable state between tests, and misconfigured mocks. Mastering Jest debugging means understanding the test lifecycle, Jest's module resolution, and how to isolate the exact line causing a failure using tools like --verbose, --detectOpenHandles, and the Node.js debugger.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, a Jest unit test for the leave-balance deduction logic started failing intermittently after adding a new "carry-forward" feature. The test deducts 3 days from balance passed in isolation (jest --testPathPattern=balance) but failed when the full suite ran. Manoj traced the issue to a shared mockPrisma object that a previous test modified without resetting — the findUnique mock still returned a carry-forward balance from the previous test instead of the expected fresh balance of 20.

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code Example

// leaveBalance.test.ts — BEFORE (flaky due to shared mock state)
import { LeaveService } from './leaveService';

const mockPrisma = {
  leaveBalance: {
    findUnique: jest.fn(),
    update: jest.fn()
  }
};

const service = new LeaveService(mockPrisma as any);

// Test A: carry-forward test modifies mock return
it('includes carry-forward in total balance', async () => {
  mockPrisma.leaveBalance.findUnique.mockResolvedValue({
    remaining: 5, carryForward: 3  // sets mock to return carryForward
  });
  const result = await service.getTotalBalance('emp-1');
  expect(result).toBe(8);
});

// Test B: FAILS because findUnique still returns carryForward data
it('deducts 3 days from balance', async () => {
  // BUG: forgot to set new mock — uses stale return from Test A
  mockPrisma.leaveBalance.update.mockResolvedValue({ remaining: 17 });
  const result = await service.deductDays('emp-1', 3);
  expect(result.remaining).toBe(17); // might pass or fail depending on order
});

// AFTER — Fixed with proper mock reset
beforeEach(() => {
  jest.clearAllMocks(); // reset all mock state between tests
});

it('deducts 3 days from balance', async () => {
  // Explicitly set mock for THIS test
  mockPrisma.leaveBalance.findUnique.mockResolvedValue({
    remaining: 20, carryForward: 0
  });
  mockPrisma.leaveBalance.update.mockResolvedValue({ remaining: 17 });
  const result = await service.deductDays('emp-1', 3);
  expect(result.remaining).toBe(17);
});

// Debugging commands:
// jest --verbose --runInBand   (run sequentially to reproduce order dependency)
// jest --detectOpenHandles     (find unclosed async operations)
// node --inspect-brk node_modules/.bin/jest --testPathPattern=balance
//   (attach VS Code debugger to step through test)

5. Debugging Narrative

Manoj's debugging journey for this HR Leave System test failure:

  1. Symptom: CI reported leaveBalance.test.ts failing with Expected: 17, Received: 8. The test passed locally.
  2. Step 1: Ran jest --runInBand --verbose locally — reproduced the failure. The --runInBand flag forced sequential execution, matching CI behavior.
  3. Step 2: Added console.log before the assertion — saw that findUnique was returning { remaining: 5, carryForward: 3 } instead of the expected { remaining: 20 }.
  4. Step 3: Searched for where that mock value was set — found Test A (carry-forward test) set it and never cleared it.
  5. Fix: Added beforeEach(() => jest.clearAllMocks()) and ensured every test explicitly sets its own mock return values.
  6. Prevention: Added ESLint rule jest/require-top-level-describe and a shared test setup file that clears mocks globally.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Tests pass in isolation but fail in suite due to shared mock stateAlways add beforeEach(() => jest.clearAllMocks()) at the top of every describe block
Async tests appear to pass but silently swallow errorsAlways await async assertions or use expect.assertions(n) to verify assertion count
Using toBe for object comparison — always passes as reference checkUse toEqual for deep equality on objects and arrays; toBe only for primitives
Jest caches transformed modules between runs causing stale codeRun jest --clearCache when tests behave unexpectedly after code changes

7. Validation Strategy

  • Order independence: Run jest --randomize to shuffle test order and verify no test depends on execution sequence
  • Assertion count: Add expect.assertions(n) in async tests to ensure all assertions execute
  • CI parity: Use --runInBand in CI config to match sequential execution and catch ordering bugs early
  • Coverage gate: Require all new test files to include beforeEach mock cleanup via a custom ESLint rule

Compare Jest Unit Tests: Behavior-Driven vs. Implementation-Driven

1. Definition & Context

There are two fundamental approaches to writing Jest unit tests: Behavior-Driven Testing (BDT) tests the public API — given specific inputs, assert expected outputs without caring how the function works internally. Implementation-Driven Testing (IDT) verifies internal mechanics — checking that specific internal methods are called, specific state transitions happen, or specific private variables are set. The choice between them determines test resilience to refactoring and maintenance cost.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the contact search feature initially used Array.filter() on cached contacts. Tests were written implementation-style, spying on Array.prototype.filter. When Manoj refactored to use MongoDB's $regex query for server-side search, all 12 search tests broke — even though the feature worked perfectly. Rewriting the tests as behavior-driven (assert that searching "John" returns contacts with "John" in the name) made them refactor-proof and reduced test maintenance by 60%.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// ContactsHub search feature — TWO testing approaches

// ========== BEHAVIOR-DRIVEN (Recommended) ==========
describe('searchContacts — behavior-driven', () => {
  const contacts = [
    { name: 'John Doe', email: 'john@test.com' },
    { name: 'Jane Smith', email: 'jane@test.com' },
    { name: 'Johnny Walker', email: 'jw@test.com' }
  ];

  it('returns contacts matching the search term', () => {
    const results = searchContacts(contacts, 'John');
    expect(results).toHaveLength(2);
    expect(results.map(c => c.name)).toEqual(['John Doe', 'Johnny Walker']);
  });

  it('returns empty array when no match found', () => {
    expect(searchContacts(contacts, 'xyz')).toEqual([]);
  });

  it('performs case-insensitive search', () => {
    expect(searchContacts(contacts, 'john')).toHaveLength(2);
  });
});

// ========== IMPLEMENTATION-DRIVEN (Fragile) ==========
describe('searchContacts — implementation-driven', () => {
  it('calls Array.filter with case-insensitive matcher', () => {
    const spy = jest.spyOn(Array.prototype, 'filter');
    searchContacts(contacts, 'John');
    expect(spy).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1);
    // BREAKS if you switch from Array.filter to MongoDB $regex!
    spy.mockRestore();
  });

  it('uses toLowerCase for case insensitivity', () => {
    const spy = jest.spyOn(String.prototype, 'toLowerCase');
    searchContacts(contacts, 'John');
    expect(spy).toHaveBeenCalled();
    // BREAKS if you switch to RegExp('john', 'i') approach!
    spy.mockRestore();
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, after Manoj migrated search from client-side filtering to server-side MongoDB $regex, the CI pipeline showed 12 test failures. All failures were in implementation-driven tests checking Array.prototype.filter calls. The feature itself worked perfectly — Manoj verified by manually testing in the browser and checking the Playwright E2E tests (which all passed because they tested behavior).

The fix took 2 hours: rewriting all 12 tests to assert on returned results rather than internal method calls. Manoj established a team convention: "If a test asserts on a spy of a built-in method (Array, String, JSON), it's probably testing implementation. Rewrite to test output." This reduced test-maintenance time in subsequent refactors by 60%.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
All tests are behavior-driven, missing critical internal contractsUse implementation tests only for external dependency calls (DB, API) where verifying the call was made is the actual requirement
Behavior tests become too broad — testing too many behaviors in one testFollow the Arrange-Act-Assert pattern with one assertion concept per test; use descriptive it('...') names
Spying on built-in prototypes (Array, String) leaks to other testsAlways call spy.mockRestore() in afterEach or avoid prototype spying entirely

7. Validation Strategy

  • Refactor resilience test: Intentionally refactor the implementation (e.g., swap filter for reduce); all behavior tests must still pass
  • Coverage check: Ensure behavior tests cover edge cases (empty input, special characters, very long strings) not just happy paths
  • PR review rule: Flag any test that uses jest.spyOn(Array.prototype, ...) or jest.spyOn(String.prototype, ...) as a code smell
  • Mutation testing: Run Stryker to verify behavior tests actually catch real bugs, not just hit code lines

Compare Mocking Strategies: Manual Mocks vs. Dependency Injection

1. Definition & Context

Two primary mocking strategies exist for isolating dependencies in tests: Manual Mocks use jest.mock() to replace module imports at the file system level — Jest intercepts require/import and returns your mock. Dependency Injection (DI) passes dependencies as constructor/function parameters, making it trivial to swap real implementations with test doubles. Each approach has distinct trade-offs in flexibility, type safety, and test maintainability.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the LeaveApprovalService depended on Prisma, Socket.io, and an email service. Initially, Manoj used jest.mock('@prisma/client') and jest.mock('../services/emailService') — file-path-based manual mocks. When the email service file was renamed from emailService.ts to notificationService.ts, all 18 tests broke because jest.mock('../services/emailService') pointed to a non-existent path. After refactoring to DI, the LeaveApprovalService constructor accepted INotifier and IDatabase interfaces — tests simply passed mock objects, and file renames never affected tests again.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// ========== APPROACH 1: jest.mock() ==========
// Tied to file path — breaks on rename or restructure
jest.mock('../services/emailService', () => ({
  sendEmail: jest.fn().mockResolvedValue(true)
}));
jest.mock('@prisma/client', () => ({
  PrismaClient: jest.fn().mockImplementation(() => mockPrisma)
}));

import { LeaveApprovalService } from './leaveApprovalService';
import { sendEmail } from '../services/emailService';

it('sends approval email', async () => {
  const service = new LeaveApprovalService();
  await service.approve(1);
  expect(sendEmail).toHaveBeenCalledWith(expect.objectContaining({
    subject: 'Leave Approved'
  }));
});

// ========== APPROACH 2: Dependency Injection ==========
// Decoupled — immune to file renames
interface INotifier {
  send(to: string, subject: string, body: string): Promise<boolean>;
}
interface IDatabase {
  leave: { findUnique: Function; update: Function };
}

class LeaveApprovalService {
  constructor(private db: IDatabase, private notifier: INotifier) {}

  async approve(leaveId: number) {
    const leave = await this.db.leave.findUnique({ where: { id: leaveId } });
    await this.db.leave.update({ where: { id: leaveId }, data: { status: 'approved' } });
    await this.notifier.send(leave.email, 'Leave Approved', 'Your leave is approved');
  }
}

// Test — no jest.mock() needed
const mockDB = {
  leave: {
    findUnique: jest.fn().mockResolvedValue({ id: 1, email: 'emp@hr.com' }),
    update: jest.fn().mockResolvedValue({ id: 1, status: 'approved' })
  }
};
const mockNotifier = { send: jest.fn().mockResolvedValue(true) };

it('sends approval notification via DI', async () => {
  const service = new LeaveApprovalService(mockDB, mockNotifier);
  await service.approve(1);
  expect(mockNotifier.send).toHaveBeenCalledWith(
    'emp@hr.com', 'Leave Approved', expect.any(String)
  );
});

5. Debugging Narrative

When Manoj refactored the HR Leave System's notification layer from a single emailService.ts to a unified notificationService.ts (supporting email + Socket.io + SMS), every test file with jest.mock('../services/emailService') failed with Cannot find module '../services/emailService'. The actual notification feature worked perfectly. Manoj spent 3 hours updating 18 test files with the new path. After this incident, he refactored all services to accept dependencies via constructors, eliminating file-path coupling in tests entirely. The next rename (splitting notificationService into emailNotifier and socketNotifier) required zero test changes.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
jest.mock() hoists to top of file — can't vary mock per testUse jest.spyOn() inside individual tests for per-test control, or use DI
DI leads to constructor with 6+ parameters ("constructor hell")Group related deps into a single config object: new Service({ db, notifier, logger })
Mixing both approaches in same codebase creates inconsistencyEstablish team convention: DI for your services, jest.mock() only for third-party node_modules

7. Validation Strategy

  • Rename resilience: Rename a service file in a feature branch — verify zero test failures if using DI
  • Mock accuracy: Use TypeScript interfaces for mock objects — compiler catches when mock shape diverges from real implementation
  • Coverage equivalence: Both approaches should produce identical code coverage — compare with jest --coverage
  • Review checklist: PR reviews flag new jest.mock() calls for internal modules — suggest DI refactor instead

Design a Mocking Strategy for a Full-Stack Application

1. Definition & Context

Designing a mocking strategy means defining what to mock, at which boundary, and with what technique across your entire test suite. A well-designed strategy creates a clear hierarchy: unit tests mock all external dependencies, integration tests mock only external services (not your own layers), and E2E tests mock nothing. The goal is fast, reliable tests that give high confidence without testing the mocks themselves.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the mock strategy was layered across three tiers. Unit tests for the Angular service layer used HttpClientTestingModule to intercept HTTP calls — testing that components called the right endpoints with correct parameters. Integration tests for the Express.js API used an in-memory MongoDB instance (mongodb-memory-server) instead of mocking Mongoose — testing real query behavior. The only external mock was the Recharts rendering library in component tests, which was replaced with a simple <div data-testid="chart" /> to avoid Canvas API dependencies in jsdom.

3. Mock Strategy Architecture

4. Code Example

// CRM Dashboard — Mock strategy across tiers

// === TIER 1: Unit test (Angular service) ===
import { TestBed } from '@angular/core/testing';
import { HttpClientTestingModule, HttpTestingController } from
  '@angular/common/http/testing';
import { DealService } from './deal.service';

describe('DealService (unit)', () => {
  let service: DealService;
  let httpMock: HttpTestingController;

  beforeEach(() => {
    TestBed.configureTestingModule({
      imports: [HttpClientTestingModule],
      providers: [DealService]
    });
    service = TestBed.inject(DealService);
    httpMock = TestBed.inject(HttpTestingController);
  });

  afterEach(() => httpMock.verify());

  it('fetches deals with stage filter', () => {
    service.getDealsByStage('negotiation').subscribe(deals => {
      expect(deals).toHaveLength(2);
    });
    const req = httpMock.expectOne('/api/deals?stage=negotiation');
    expect(req.request.method).toBe('GET');
    req.flush([{ id: 1, stage: 'negotiation' }, { id: 2, stage: 'negotiation' }]);
  });
});

// === TIER 2: Integration test (Express + MongoDB) ===
import { MongoMemoryServer } from 'mongodb-memory-server';
import request from 'supertest';
import { app } from '../app';

let mongoServer: MongoMemoryServer;

beforeAll(async () => {
  mongoServer = await MongoMemoryServer.create();
  process.env.MONGO_URI = mongoServer.getUri();
  await app.connectDB();
});

afterAll(async () => {
  await mongoServer.stop();
});

it('creates deal and returns with generated ID', async () => {
  const res = await request(app)
    .post('/api/deals')
    .send({ title: 'Acme Contract', value: 50000, stage: 'proposal' });
  expect(res.status).toBe(201);
  expect(res.body._id).toBeDefined();
  expect(res.body.stage).toBe('proposal');
});

// === TIER 3: E2E test (Playwright — no mocks) ===
// See Playwright tests in ContactsHub — real browser, real API, real DB

5. Debugging Narrative

In the CRM Dashboard, Manoj discovered that unit tests for the pipeline chart component passed with flying colors, but the actual chart showed wrong data in the browser. The issue: HttpClientTestingModule mock returned deals as [{ stage: 'negotiation' }] — but the real API returned { data: [{ stage: 'negotiation' }], total: 1 } with a wrapper object. The mock didn't match the real response shape. Manoj fixed this by creating typed mock factories that matched the actual API contract: function mockDealsResponse(deals: Deal[]): ApiResponse<Deal[]>. He also added a contract test that validated the mock factory output against the real API's JSON schema.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Mock return values don't match real API response shapeCreate typed mock factories with TypeScript interfaces; validate against JSON schema from API docs
Over-mocking in integration tests reduces confidenceIntegration tests should use real DB (in-memory) and only mock truly external services (email, S3)
Maintaining three tiers of tests for every feature is expensiveNot every feature needs all three tiers — critical user journeys get E2E, all logic gets unit tests, API boundaries get integration

7. Validation Strategy

  • Mock shape validation: Compare mock factory output against real API response using a shared Zod schema
  • Test pyramid ratio: Maintain ~70% unit / 20% integration / 10% E2E test ratio; review in sprint retrospectives
  • Mock freshness: When API contracts change, update mock factories first — type errors surface immediately if tests use typed factories
  • Confidence audit: Periodically disable mocks in integration tests to verify real dependencies still match mock behavior

Compare Integration Testing Approaches: Supertest vs. Testcontainers

1. Definition & Context

Two popular approaches for integration testing backend APIs are: Supertest with in-memory databases — using libraries like mongodb-memory-server or SQLite in-memory to simulate the database layer while testing real HTTP handlers — and Testcontainers — spinning up actual Docker containers (PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis) for each test suite, providing 100% database fidelity. The trade-off is speed vs. accuracy: in-memory databases are faster but may behave differently from production databases, while containers are slower but test against the real database engine.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the leave-overlap detection used a PostgreSQL-specific DATERANGE type with && (overlap) operator in a raw Prisma query. When testing with SQLite in-memory (via Prisma's multi-provider support), the overlap query always returned false because SQLite doesn't support DATERANGE. This created a dangerous false positive — tests passed, but the overlap detection didn't actually work. Switching to Testcontainers with a real PostgreSQL image caught the bug immediately and provided true confidence in the overlap logic.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// === APPROACH 1: Supertest + mongodb-memory-server ===
import { MongoMemoryServer } from 'mongodb-memory-server';
import request from 'supertest';
import { buildApp } from '../app';

let mongod: MongoMemoryServer;
let app: any;

beforeAll(async () => {
  mongod = await MongoMemoryServer.create();
  process.env.MONGO_URI = mongod.getUri();
  app = await buildApp();
});

afterAll(async () => {
  await app.close();
  await mongod.stop();
});

it('creates and retrieves a contact', async () => {
  const create = await request(app.server)
    .post('/api/contacts')
    .send({ name: 'John', email: 'john@test.com' });
  expect(create.status).toBe(201);

  const get = await request(app.server).get(`/api/contacts/${create.body._id}`);
  expect(get.body.name).toBe('John');
});

// === APPROACH 2: Testcontainers + real PostgreSQL ===
import { PostgreSqlContainer } from '@testcontainers/postgresql';
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';
import { buildApp } from '../app';

let container: any;
let prisma: PrismaClient;

beforeAll(async () => {
  container = await new PostgreSqlContainer('postgres:15')
    .withDatabase('hr_test')
    .start();
  process.env.DATABASE_URL = container.getConnectionUri();
  prisma = new PrismaClient();
  // Run Prisma migrations against the container
  await exec('npx prisma migrate deploy');
}, 30000); // 30s timeout for container startup

afterAll(async () => {
  await prisma.$disconnect();
  await container.stop();
});

it('detects overlapping leave with DATERANGE operator', async () => {
  await prisma.leave.create({ data: {
    employeeId: 1, startDate: '2025-06-01', endDate: '2025-06-05', status: 'approved'
  }});
  // This query uses PostgreSQL-specific DATERANGE && operator
  const overlap = await prisma.$queryRaw`
    SELECT id FROM leaves
    WHERE daterange("startDate", "endDate", '[]') &&
          daterange(${new Date('2025-06-03')}::date, ${new Date('2025-06-10')}::date, '[]')
  `;
  expect(overlap).toHaveLength(1);
});

5. Debugging Narrative

In the HR Leave System, Manoj's test suite using SQLite in-memory had 100% pass rate for months. But in production, users reported they could create overlapping leave requests. Debugging revealed the DATERANGE overlap query silently fell back to a string comparison in SQLite, which always returned no overlap. Manoj added console.log to the raw query result — empty array in tests, correct results against real PostgreSQL. He switched the overlap-related tests to Testcontainers while keeping simpler CRUD tests on in-memory SQLite for speed. The hybrid approach maintained fast CI times (~45 seconds) while catching database-specific bugs.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
In-memory DB gives false positives for DB-specific features (DATERANGE, JSON operators, full-text search)Use Testcontainers for tests that exercise DB-specific features; keep in-memory for simple CRUD
Testcontainers slow CI by 2-3 minutes due to Docker image pullPre-cache Docker images in CI, or use withReuse(true) to reuse containers across test suites
Testcontainers require Docker — may not be available in all CI environmentsAdd a CI-specific flag to skip Testcontainer tests and run only in-memory; run full suite in nightly builds

7. Validation Strategy

  • Parity check: Run the same test against both in-memory and Testcontainers — any result difference reveals a DB fidelity gap
  • CI performance budget: Integration tests must complete within 90 seconds; Testcontainer tests must complete within 3 minutes
  • Docker health: CI pipeline step verifies Docker daemon is running before Testcontainer tests; skip gracefully if unavailable
  • Query audit: Any raw SQL query in the codebase must have a corresponding Testcontainer test, not just in-memory

Design an Integration Testing Strategy for a Full-Stack Application

1. Definition & Context

An integration testing strategy defines how to verify that multiple components of your application work together correctly — routes + services + database, frontend + API, or service + external APIs. Unlike unit tests that isolate one function, integration tests exercise real interactions between layers. A well-designed strategy balances test speed, reliability, and the scope of what each test covers to maximize confidence per test-second spent.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the integration testing strategy covered three critical boundaries: (1) API layer: Fastify route handlers + MongoDB via mongodb-memory-server — testing that CRUD endpoints correctly validate input, persist to the database, and return proper HTTP responses. (2) File upload pipeline: Cloudinary mock + Multer middleware — testing that avatar uploads are processed, resized, and stored with the correct metadata. (3) CSV import: Testing the bulk import endpoint with real CSV parsing and database insertion of 500 records, verifying duplicate detection and error reporting. These 25 integration tests caught bugs that unit tests missed — like a Mongoose validation error that only triggered when creating contacts with special characters in the email field.

3. Integration Test Architecture

4. Code Example

// ContactsHub — Integration test setup + CRUD test
import { MongoMemoryServer } from 'mongodb-memory-server';
import { buildApp } from '../app';
import { FastifyInstance } from 'fastify';

let app: FastifyInstance;
let mongod: MongoMemoryServer;

beforeAll(async () => {
  mongod = await MongoMemoryServer.create();
  process.env.MONGO_URI = mongod.getUri();
  process.env.CLOUDINARY_URL = 'mock://test'; // prevents real uploads
  app = await buildApp();
});

afterAll(async () => {
  await app.close();
  await mongod.stop();
});

afterEach(async () => {
  // Clean all collections between tests for isolation
  const collections = await app.mongo.db.collections();
  for (const col of collections) {
    await col.deleteMany({});
  }
});

describe('POST /api/contacts', () => {
  it('creates contact with valid data', async () => {
    const res = await app.inject({
      method: 'POST',
      url: '/api/contacts',
      payload: { name: 'Alice', email: 'alice@test.com', phone: '+91 9876543210' }
    });
    expect(res.statusCode).toBe(201);
    expect(JSON.parse(res.body)).toMatchObject({
      name: 'Alice',
      email: 'alice@test.com'
    });
  });

  it('rejects duplicate email with 409', async () => {
    // Create first contact
    await app.inject({
      method: 'POST', url: '/api/contacts',
      payload: { name: 'Alice', email: 'alice@test.com' }
    });
    // Attempt duplicate
    const res = await app.inject({
      method: 'POST', url: '/api/contacts',
      payload: { name: 'Bob', email: 'alice@test.com' }
    });
    expect(res.statusCode).toBe(409);
  });

  it('validates email format', async () => {
    const res = await app.inject({
      method: 'POST', url: '/api/contacts',
      payload: { name: 'Bad', email: 'not-an-email' }
    });
    expect(res.statusCode).toBe(400);
  });
});

describe('POST /api/contacts/import-csv', () => {
  it('imports 500 contacts from CSV file', async () => {
    const csvBuffer = generateTestCSV(500);
    const res = await app.inject({
      method: 'POST',
      url: '/api/contacts/import-csv',
      payload: csvBuffer,
      headers: { 'content-type': 'text/csv' }
    });
    expect(res.statusCode).toBe(200);
    expect(JSON.parse(res.body).imported).toBe(500);
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, Manoj noticed integration tests for the CSV import endpoint were intermittently failing with MongoServerError: E11000 duplicate key. The test imported 500 contacts, but some CSV rows had emails that differed only in case ("John@Test.com" vs "john@test.com"). MongoDB's unique index was case-sensitive by default, but the application's email normalization (toLowerCase) ran after the unique check in the Mongoose pre-save hook. Manoj fixed the issue by adding a case-insensitive collation to the MongoDB unique index: { email: 1 }, { unique: true, collation: { locale: 'en', strength: 2 } }. This bug would have been invisible in unit tests — only the integration test with real MongoDB caught the collation issue.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Integration tests that depend on execution orderClear database collections in afterEach; each test creates its own seed data
In-memory MongoDB doesn't replicate all production behaviors (transactions, sharding)Use mongodb-memory-server with the same MongoDB version as production; add explicit tests for replica-set features if needed
Cloudinary/S3 calls in integration tests hit real external servicesMock external HTTP calls with nock or set environment flags to use local file storage in test mode

7. Validation Strategy

  • Isolation check: Run each test file independently (jest --testPathPattern=file) and in full suite — results must match
  • Performance budget: All integration tests must finish within 60 seconds; alert if any single test exceeds 5 seconds
  • Error path coverage: Every endpoint must have at least one happy-path and one error-path integration test (400, 401, 409, etc.)
  • Data cleanup audit: CI step that checks for leftover documents in the test database after the suite completes — zero documents expected
